behaviors.description: Generated reply references specific points from the incoming email rather than producing a generic response.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: A unit test could verify that the email thread is passed to the LLM prompt, but cannot verify the LLM actually uses that content. LLM-DEP: Whether the reply contains thread-specific references versus boilerplate is entirely determined by real LLM behavior. A deterministic stub would return a canned response that either always passes or always fails, hiding genuine model failures. REJECTION-GATE: No behavior text limits this to prompt-construction logic alone — the claim is about the generated reply's content. COMMIT-TO-REASONING: Evidence points to Agentic; output is Agentic.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a multi-point email thread containing specific named entities, dates, and explicit requests to the real LLM draft generator. Assert that the generated reply contains at least one verbatim or clearly paraphrased reference to a thread-specific detail (e.g., the named entity or date), ruling out a purely generic opener/closer as the only content.

behaviors.description: Factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that contradict entries in the knowledge base.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is that the checker fails to detect a semantic contradiction — not just that it fails to call the KB. This requires real reasoning about claim vs. KB entry meaning. LLM-DEP: 'Flags claims that contradict' implies semantic contradiction detection; in an LLM-based assistant context this is almost certainly LLM- or embedding-model-powered, not simple string matching. A stub checker would always return a fixed flag count, hiding failures where the model misses subtle contradictions. MOCK-HIDE: Any stub that returns canned flags always passes, hiding real classification failures. REJECTION-GATE: Nothing in the behavior limits the checker to deterministic rule matching. COMMIT-TO-REASONING: Evidence points to Agentic; output is Agentic.
behaviors.test_description: Seed a knowledge base with entries containing specific, unambiguous factual claims (e.g., 'Product X supports a maximum of 10 users'). Generate or inject a draft reply asserting a directly contradicting claim (e.g., 'Product X supports up to 50 users'). Run the real factual consistency checker. Assert that the returned flags include at least one entry identifying the contradicting claim and referencing the KB entry it conflicts with.

behaviors.description: When the knowledge base has no relevant entries, the draft includes a disclaimer rather than fabricating facts.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: A unit test can verify an empty-KB signal is injected into the prompt, but cannot verify the LLM honors it. LLM-DEP: Whether the real LLM produces a disclaimer vs. a hallucinated answer when given an empty KB context is purely a function of the model's behavior under that prompt condition. A stub always returns a canned disclaimer, trivially passing regardless of actual LLM behavior. REJECTION-GATE: The failure of interest ('fabricating facts') is an LLM-output failure; no behavior text restricts this to prompt-engineering or routing logic. COMMIT-TO-REASONING: Evidence points to Agentic; output is Agentic.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the system with a knowledge base containing zero entries. Submit an email that asks for a specific factual claim (e.g., pricing, technical spec, policy). Run the real LLM draft generator. Assert that the draft contains an explicit uncertainty marker or disclaimer phrase (e.g., 'I don't have verified information on…', 'I cannot confirm…') and does not assert any specific factual value for the requested information.

behaviors.description: The approval workflow blocks sending until the user explicitly approves — no draft is sent automatically.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure — sending without approval — is a state/control-flow bug at the boundary between the approval module and the send module, not inside a single function. A unit test of the send function alone cannot catch a caller that bypasses the approval gate. REAL-INFRA: The blocking behavior does not require real email delivery infrastructure; a spy/stub on the send function captures whether it was invoked. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) No real OS/network needed — the test only needs to observe whether the send call is gated; a stub send captures this. (b) No LLM dependency. (c) No chained agentic steps. LLM-DEP: None — this is pure control-flow. COMMIT-TO-REASONING: Evidence points to Integration; output is Integration.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the approval module to a stub send function. Trigger the complete draft pipeline (using a deterministic stub LLM) up to the approval-pending state. Assert that the stub send function has not been called. Then invoke the user-approval action. Assert that the stub send function is called exactly once, with the approved draft as its argument. Also assert that calling the pipeline a second time without re-approving does not trigger a second send.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — read thread, generate draft, check facts, present for approval — works end-to-end for a real email thread.
behaviors.justification: MULTI-STEP: The behavior explicitly names four sequential stages, two of which involve real LLM calls (draft generation, factual consistency check). End-to-end failure modes include context loss between steps, approval state corruption, and LLM output from step N mishandled by step N+1 — none of which appear in single-step tests. LLM-DEP: At minimum the draft-generation step depends on real LLM output; the factual check may as well. MOCK-HIDE: Stubbing any step severs the handoff that this test exists to verify. REJECTION-GATE: The behavior says 'full sequence … for a real email thread' — nothing in the text limits it to a subset of steps. COMMIT-TO-REASONING: Evidence points to Workflow; output is Workflow.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a realistic multi-message email thread fixture and a populated knowledge base. Invoke the full pipeline (read thread → LLM draft generation → factual consistency check → approval gate) against the real LLM. Assert that each stage returns a structurally valid output, that the consistency check result references the KB, that the draft is surfaced for approval before any send action is triggered, and that the end-to-end output coherently responds to the thread content. Run at least 3 representative threads to surface non-determinism failures.
